sample.title: The Woman King
sample.plot: A historical epic inspired by true events that took place in The Kingdom of Dahomey, one of the most powerful states of Africa in the 18th and 19th centuries.

sample.title: The Woman King
sample.plot: A historical epic inspired by true events that took place in The Kingdom of Dahomey, one of the most powerful states of Africa in the 18th and 19th centuries.

categories.label: kingdoms centralising power through warfare
categories.description: In parts of West Africa, strong kingdoms gather people, tribute, and captives under a ruler whose power rests on conquest, court authority, and command of armed force. What you'll see: palace compounds, royal processions, tribute brought before the throne, and war councils shaping the life of the state.

categories.label: atlantic slave trading pressing on inland states
categories.description: European demand for captives at the coast bends politics inland, tying diplomacy, raiding, and royal wealth to the buying and selling of human beings. What you'll see: coastal forts and traders, negotiations over captives, caravans under guard, and markets where war and commerce meet.

categories.label: royal courts ordering rank and tribute
categories.description: Government is personal and ceremonial, with the palace serving as the centre of justice, taxation, ritual, and favour rather than a distant written bureaucracy. What you'll see: audiences before the ruler, elaborate court etiquette, gift exchange, officials enforcing orders, and wealth displayed in cloth, metal, and carved regalia.

categories.label: women holding formal place in arms
categories.description: Some African states make room for women within royal and military institutions, linking service, discipline, and status directly to the crown. What you'll see: training grounds, barracks life, ceremonial inspections, strict hierarchies of command, and women moving in spaces of power usually reserved for armed retainers.

categories.label: religion joining ancestors, spirits, and rule
categories.description: Belief is woven through government and daily life, with ancestors, local divinities, oaths, and ritual acts giving moral force to kingship and war. What you'll see: shrines, offerings, divination, sacred taboos, ritual before battle, and public ceremonies where politics and worship are inseparable.

categories.label: trade routes tying coast to interior
categories.description: Goods, news, and people move along established routes between inland capitals and Atlantic ports, linking local production to wider exchange. What you'll see: caravans, porters bearing goods, imported cloth and firearms beside local wares, and bustling markets where many languages and interests meet.

categories.label: firearms changing the balance of war
categories.description: Gunpowder weapons do not erase older fighting methods, but they alter tactics, prestige, and the terms on which states bargain with neighbours and foreign merchants. What you'll see: muskets alongside blades and spears, drilling and powder stores, volleys in battle, and the visible value placed on weapons from overseas.

categories.label: captivity and slavery structuring social order
categories.description: Unfreedom exists within the region as well as across the ocean, shaping households, labour, military service, and the fate of those taken in war. What you'll see: captives guarded after raids, enslaved labour within compounds and fields, household dependants of different rank, and constant attention to who is free and who is owned.

categories.label: village farming supporting royal capitals
categories.description: Even powerful courts depend on surrounding farming communities that raise food, pay dues, and supply labour to sustain armies and palaces. What you'll see: yam and grain cultivation, cooking and pounding food, village compounds, seasonal rhythms of work, and stores of provisions feeding the state.

categories.label: foreign merchants bargained with warily
categories.description: European traders are influential at the coast but must deal with African rulers as negotiating powers, not as masters of the interior. What you'll see: tense bargaining, interpreters and middlemen, gifts and demands, coastal enclaves marked by foreign goods, and mutual suspicion beneath formal courtesy.
